sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content:
Unopposed Estrogen Exposure (Hormonal, Strong): Increased levels of endogenous or exogenous estrogen without opposing progesterone is a primary risk factor. This includes obesity (peripheral conversion in adipose tissue), chronic anovulation, polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS), and exogenous estrogen therapy without progestin.

Obesity (Metabolic, Strong): A major risk factor. Fat cells convert androgens to estrogens via aromatase. The incidence of endometrial cancer increases with body mass index (BMI).

Diabetes Mellitus (Metabolic, Moderate): An independent risk factor, likely related to hyperinsulinemia, which may promote endometrial proliferation.

Hypertension (Metabolic, Moderate): Often associated with obesity and diabetes, and is an independent risk factor.

Lynch Syndrome (Genetic, Strong): Hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC) syndrome, caused by germline mutations in mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Carriers have a lifetime risk of endometrial cancer of up to 60%. Endometrial cancer may be the first manifestation.

Tamoxifen Use (Pharmacologic, Moderate): Selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) used for breast cancer treatment and prevention. It has agonist effects on the endometrium, increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer with prolonged use (typically >2 years).

Nulliparity (Reproductive, Moderate): Never having been pregnant. Pregnancy has a protective effect due to progestin dominance.

Early Menarche / Late Menopause (Reproductive, Moderate): Prolonged exposure to cyclic estrogen without the protective effect of progesterone from ovulation.

Age (Demographic, Strong): The risk increases with age, with most cases diagnosed after age 55.

Endometrial Hyperplasia with Atypia (Premalignant, Strong): Atypical endometrial hyperplasia is a direct precursor lesion for endometrioid adenocarcinoma.

Family History (Genetic, Moderate): A family history of endometrial, ovarian, or colorectal cancer, even in the absence of known Lynch syndrome, may increase risk.
